event_blocks.summary: An upbeat electronic music score accompanies the opening, which features a man with a camera focusing on its lens against a blurred outdoor backdrop, establishing a photography theme. This transitions to a top-down view of a desk laden with photography and design tools, including a laptop, camera lenses, color swatches, and markers, while a text overlay declares 'The creative photo-editing software designed by photographers, for photographers.' The scene then shifts to a woman in a vibrant, neon-lit urban setting, seated on a textured floor, with text highlighting '8 software' and listing tools such as Nik Color Efex, Nik Silver Efex, and Nik Viveza. A male voice begins to speak, stating, "We're going to be discussing precise editing made simple, using Nik Viveza." The visual then changes to a webinar slide titled 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos,' presented by Dan Hughes, with a circular editing interface superimposed on the woman’s image. The speaker elaborates, "This is the stuff we're going to talk about basically today... in a nutshell, we're talking about controlling light and color using Viveza," explaining that editing can be performed "globally... adjusting the entire image all at once, and we can do it selectively... on particular objects or in particular areas, based upon where we place control points." A static slide with orange and black text detailing Nik Viveza’s capabilities—precise color manipulation using Control Points, new presets, and a redesigned interface—emphasizes ease and control in editing outdoor photos. The speaker concludes, "If you're brand new to the Nik Collection, no worries, we're going to be discussing what a control point is..." The hexagonal orange and black Nik Collection logo is consistently visible in the bottom right corner during the latter part of the presentation.

Webinar Title Slide.description: A dark-themed slide with the Nik Collection 4 logo, 'WEBINAR' label, and title 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos,' presented by Dan Hughes, with an editing interface overlay on the woman’s image, directly introducing the topic being discussed by the male speaker.

Nik Collection Logo.description: A hexagonal orange and black logo with a stylized camera aperture, consistently appearing in the bottom right corner of the frames, and its software suite is the subject of the male speaker's discussion, particularly for new users.

Woman in Neon Scene.description: A woman in a red outfit sitting on a textured floor in a vibrant, neon-lit urban environment, with a circular editing interface overlay showing sliders for Br, Co, Sa, St, and Sh, serving as the visual example for the editing techniques discussed.

Man with Camera.description: A man in a blue shirt holding a DSLR camera, focusing on the lens with a blurred natural landscape in the background.

Text Overlay.description: White and orange text describing the webinar and Nik Viveza’s features, including phrases like 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos' and details about color presets and Control Points, which are the core topics of the male speaker's instructional presentation.

Desk with Tools.description: A top-down view of a white desk cluttered with photography and design tools, including a laptop, camera lenses, color swatches, markers, and a color wheel, with text overlay promoting Nik Collection.

This transitions to a top-down view of a desk cluttered with photography and design tools, including a laptop, camera lenses, color swatches, and markers, accompanied by a text overlay stating 'The creative photo-editing software designed by photographers, for photographers.' The scene then shifts to a vibrant, neon-lit urban environment featuring a woman sitting on a textured floor, with text highlighting '8 software' and listing tools like Nik Color Efex, Nik Silver Efex, and Nik Viveza. As the background music continues subtly, a male voice begins speaking, stating, "We're going to be discussing precise editing made simple, using Nik Viveza." The visual transitions to a webinar slide titled 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos,' presented by Dan Hughes, with a circular editing interface overlay on the woman’s image. The speaker elaborates, "This is the stuff we're going to talk about basically today... in a nutshell, we're talking about controlling light and color using Viveza." He continues, explaining that editing can be done "globally... adjusting the entire image all at once, and we can do it selectively... on particular objects or in particular areas, based upon where we place control points." event_blocks.summary: The tutorial begins with a title slide introducing Nik Viveza for precise editing, followed by a male speaker explaining its function and the new interface of Viveza 3, promising tips for all user levels. The scene shifts to a screen recording of DxO PhotoLab 4, a raw processor, displaying a close-up of green foliage with white flowers. The speaker details his typical workflow using raw files with processors like PhotoLab, Adobe Camera Raw, or Lightroom before Viveza, but clarifies this demonstration uses a TIFF file, indicated in the lower left. He then navigates DxO PhotoLab 4, revealing EXIF data like camera model (NIKON Z 7), ISO 800, and shutter speed 1/200 s, while noting the file type is TIF, emphasizing the flexibility of working with both raw and TIF files for later reprocessing. The speaker announces the transition to Viveza, confirming it will be launched from PhotoLab 4 and mentioning the possibility of launching from Adobe Photoshop Creative Cloud on his Mac. The Nik Collection logo is visible, and clicking it opens a Plugin Selector dialog where 'Viveza 3' is chosen. The speaker explains PhotoLab creates a duplicate for non-destructive editing in Viveza, which then launches, presenting a 'Things to know before working with a TIFF file' pop-up about resuming edits later. The Viveza 3 interface shows a split before-and-after view, with presets on the left and adjustments on the right. The pop-up is dismissed by clicking 'Do not show again' and 'OK', revealing the full split-screen view and adjustment options. The speaker demonstrates switching between single image, compare, and before/after views, highlighting the utility of the single image view for detailed inspection. He then introduces Viveza 3's presets, explaining they can save control points and that '000 Neutral' acts as a reset. Selecting a preset triggers a warning dialog about replacing parameters, which is confirmed by clicking 'Yes'. The speaker demonstrates various presets, noting they serve as starting points, and shows how to adjust global settings like Contrast and Highlights to refine the image. He also demonstrates creating and applying custom presets like 'Color IR' and 'High Key', explaining the infrared effect and refining a high-key image by adjusting brightness and highlights. Finally, the speaker details how to export, delete, and update existing presets, demonstrating the update process with the 'High Key' preset, which involves confirming a warning dialog to replace the preset with current settings, resulting in updated global and selective tone adjustments.

Text Overlay.description: White and orange text describing the webinar and Nik Viveza’s features, including phrases like 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos' and details about color presets and Control Points, which the speaker introduces as the subject of the tutorial, promising to cover the 'new interface' of Viveza.

DxO PhotoLab 4 Interface.description: A dark-themed photo editing software interface displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers, featuring panels for histogram, metadata, and basic adjustment tools on the left and right sides, with the image centered in the workspace. The Metadata panel is expanded and collapsed during the segment, revealing detailed EXIF information including camera model (NIKON Z 7), ISO 800, shutter speed 1/200 s, focal length 70 mm, and file path. The Basic Tools panel remains unchanged with all sliders at default values. The History panel at the bottom shows thumbnails of the current image and two other files. The Nik Collection logo is consistently visible in the bottom right corner, and the 'Export to Disk' button is present at the bottom right of the interface.

Nik Collection Logo.description: A hexagonal orange and black logo with a stylized camera aperture, consistently appearing in the bottom right corner of the frames. A male voice instructs the user to 'click on the Nik Collection button' to open the Plugin Selector dialog.

Plugin Selector Dialog.description: A dark-themed pop-up window listing Nik Collection plugins such as Analog Efex Pro 2, Color Efex Pro 4, Dfine2, HDR Efex Pro 2, Sharpener Pro 3 Output Sharpening, Sharpener Pro 3 Raw Presharpening, Perspective Efex, Viveza 3, Silver Efex Pro 3, and Settings. A male voice describes this as the place to 'access whichever of the Nik plugins we want to utilize,' with 'Viveza 3' being selected by the user.

Viveza 3 Interface.description: A photo editing environment with a full-screen view of a foliage image, featuring a left panel with presets ('Color IR', 'High Key') and a right panel with adjustment options. The bottom status bar indicates the file being modified is '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (24.0 MP, ISO 800, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON Z 7). The user interacts with the 'Global Adjustments' section, adjusting the Brightness slider from 11% to -6%, and the 'Selective Tones' section, adjusting the Highlights slider from 35% to 15%. The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons are visible at the bottom right. A male speaker's voice provides a continuous instructional narrative, explaining the interface's functionalities like exporting, trashing, and updating presets, directly aligning with the visual actions and tooltips shown.

Warning Dialog Box.description: A dark-themed pop-up window appearing in the center of the screen with the message 'Are you sure you want to replace this preset?' and two buttons: 'No' (gray) and 'Yes' (orange). The male speaker's voice directly anticipates and confirms the interaction with this dialog box, stating, 'it's going to say, are you sure? And um, I'm just going to say yes,' which perfectly matches the visual appearance and the user's subsequent click on 'Yes' to confirm the action.

raw_segments: The video opens with a static title slide, featuring the bold orange and black text 'Precise Editing Made Simple Using Nik Viveza on Outdoor Photos,' accompanied by a detailed description of Nik Viveza’s capabilities for selective color editing and the Nik Collection 4 logo in the bottom right. A male speaker immediately begins, explaining how Nik Viveza works, promising examples, and addressing both seasoned users and newcomers to the Nik Collection with tips and tricks, specifically mentioning that the tutorial will cover the 'new interface' within the latest version of Viveza. The scene then transitions to a screen recording of the dark-themed DxO PhotoLab 4 software, which the speaker identifies as a 'raw processor' he would typically utilize. The interface displays a close-up photograph of vibrant green foliage with white flowers, centered in the workspace, flanked by editing tools, histogram, and metadata panels. The speaker details his usual workflow involving raw files and a raw processor like PhotoLab 4, Adobe Camera Raw, or Lightroom, before processing in Viveza. He then clarifies that for this specific demonstration, he is using a 'TIFF file,' directing viewers to follow his (currently idle) cursor to the lower left corner where this file type is indicated.

The video opens on the dark-themed DxO PhotoLab 4 Interface, displaying a static close-up image of lush green foliage with clusters of white flowers. On the left, panels for Histogram, Move/Zoom, and Metadata are visible, with the Metadata panel briefly expanding to reveal detailed EXIF data including the camera model (NIKON Z 7), ISO 800, shutter speed 1/200 s, focal length 70 mm, file path, date shot (July 13, 2020), and lens details (24-70mm f/4). A male speaker's voice begins, stating he "can't exactly recall why this file is TIF," but quickly adds that "it doesn't really matter because it's going to work the same way," emphasizing the benefit of working with "raw file and or from TIF files" to "easily go back afterwards and reprocess the image." The right side of the interface shows the Basic Tools panel, with sliders for White Balance, Exposure, Highlights, Midtones, Shadows, and Blacks, all set to default values. The speaker then transitions, announcing, "let's go ahead and just jump into Vivesa," and confirms he "is going to be launching from PhotoLab 4," also mentioning the intention to launch "Vivesa from Photoshop" (specifically Adobe Photoshop Creative Cloud) and noting that he is "on a Mac." The History panel at the bottom displays thumbnails of the current image alongside 'RIT_7357.tif' and 'Slides f-002.jpeg', while the Nik Collection logo and 'Export to Disk' button remain consistently visible. The speaker concludes by reiterating, "we're here in PhotoLab, we are ready to launch our image into Vivesa."

The video opens with the DxO PhotoLab 4 Interface, displaying a close-up of green foliage with white flowers. The interface shows panels for histogram, metadata (expanded to reveal NIKON Z 7, ISO 800, 1/200 s, 70 mm details), and basic adjustment tools, with the image centered. A male voice instructs the viewer to "follow my cursor into the lower right corner here, click on the Nik Collection button." The Nik Collection logo is visible, and upon clicking it, a Plugin Selector dialog box appears, listing various Nik Collection plugins. The voice explains that from this "plugin selector," they can "access whichever of the Nik plugins we want to utilize." The user then selects 'Viveza 3', with the voice stating, "So we'll click on Viveza 3." The voice further explains that "PhotoLab is going to make a duplicate copy of our image" before "Viveza... is going to launch." The interface transitions to the Viveza 3 editing environment, where a pop-up message titled 'Things to know before working with a TIFF file' appears. The male voice clarifies that this message refers to a "relatively new feature in the Nik Collection" allowing for a "non-destructive process," meaning users can "save the file and then come back" to resume editing later. The Viveza 3 interface shows a split before-and-after view of the foliage image, with presets like '000 Neutral' and '001 Golden Hour' on the left, and adjustment options such as Global Adjustments and Control Points on the right. The bottom status bar indicates the file '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, along with camera details, while the pop-up message remains on screen.

The segment opens with the Viveza 3 photo editing interface displaying a continuous split-screen before-and-after comparison of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker explains the software's capability for non-destructive editing, stating that users can "open up the Nik Collection again and reprocess, move control points, make control point adjustments, make global adjustments around" to "reprocess the image" at a later time. Prominently displayed in the center is a pop-up dialog titled 'Things to know before working with a TIFF file', which the speaker identifies as conveying this information. He elaborates on how to dismiss the dialog permanently by clicking the 'Do not show again' checkbox and then 'OK', but notes he keeps it visible for the webinar audience. The dialog itself explains that since the image was imported in TIFF format, editing can be resumed later by enabling an option via a checkbox in the image information. The user's cursor moves over the dialog, hovering near the 'OK' button and the 'Do not show again' checkbox. At 0:54, the speaker announces, "Okay, I'm going to click the OK button," and at 0:55, the user clicks 'OK', causing the dialog to disappear. This action reveals the full split-screen view of the image, with a left panel displaying presets such as '000 Neutral', '001 Golden Hour', and '002 Blue Hour', and a right panel showing adjustment options including Loupe, Histogram, Global Adjustments, White Balance, Selective Adjustments, Control Points, and Levels & Curves. The bottom status bar confirms the file being modified is '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (24.0 MP, ISO 800, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON Z 7). The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons remain visible at the bottom right throughout the segment, as the speaker concludes by stating, "we launched into Viveza."

The segment opens with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a split-screen comparison of a foliage image with white flowers, showing the original on the left and a slightly adjusted version on the right. A male speaker explains that the last time he used Viveza, he ended up on this split or side-by-side preview to view the image before and after, though he notes it currently makes little sense as there's no difference between the two. The left panel lists presets including '000 Neutral', '001 Golden Hour', and '002 Blue Hour', while the right panel shows adjustment options like 'Loupe', 'Histogram', 'Global Adjustments', 'White Balance', 'Selective Adjustments', 'Control Points', and 'Levels & Curves'. The bottom status bar indicates the file being modified is '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (24.0 MP, ISO 800, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON Z 7). At 0:17, the user clicks the 'Compare' icon, switching the view to a full-screen display of the image. The speaker states he's going to move into the upper left quadrant of the interface and click on the single image view. The user then hovers over the 'Compare' icon again at 0:20, and at 0:24, clicks the 'Before/After' icon to toggle back to the split-screen view. The speaker clarifies that clicking the single image view provides a larger representation of the image, making it easier to see what's happening. The user continues to interact with the interface, hovering over the 'Compare' icon at 0:30 and clicking it again at 0:35 to return to the full-screen view. The speaker mentions they will visit other options and discuss zoom features, but for now, they'll stick with the single image view. He adds that he wants to cover interface elements before delving into color control, especially for new users or those who haven't updated the software. The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons are visible, and the 'Things to know before working with a TIFF file' pop-up is not present.

The video opens with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a close-up of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker immediately introduces presets as a 'great new feature' within Viveza 3, explaining that they are also found in other Nik tools and now allow saving control points. On the left panel, the user hovers over '000 Neutral' and scrolls through presets like '001 Golden Hour', '002 Blue Hour', and '003 Remove Color Cast'. The speaker clarifies that '000 Neutral' acts as a 'reset button' for the image. As the user selects '000 Neutral', a warning dialog box appears centrally, stating, 'Adding a preset will replace all parameters currently applied to your image. Would you like to continue?' The speaker's voice confirms this, explaining that clicking a preset 'is going to replace the parameters' and 'make some of the adjustments to the tools on the right-hand side of the interface.' The user clicks 'Yes' to confirm, and the interface remains full-screen, showing 'Compare' and 'Before/After' icons, with the bottom status bar indicating '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab and camera details. The right panel displays expanded 'Control Points' among other adjustment options, with 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons visible.

The segment begins with the distinct sound of a mouse click as the user confirms a 'Warning' dialog box by selecting 'Yes', a choice audibly confirmed by a male speaker stating "click yes." This dialog box, centrally displayed, warns that "Adding a preset will replace all parameters currently applied to your image. Would you like to continue?" The video showcases the Viveza 3 photo editing software, where a close-up image of green foliage with white and beige flowers is being edited. The speaker, with a clear, instructional tone, explains that the larger preview image instantly updates to match the smaller heads-up display. He clarifies that the software comes with 10 built-in presets in total, plus a neutral option, after briefly correcting himself from an earlier misstatement, and notes that users can also create their own. Throughout the minute, the user navigates the left panel of the Viveza 3 interface, which lists presets such as '000 Neutral', '001 Golden Hour', '002 Blue Hour', '003 Remove Color Cast', '004 Fill Light', '005 Darken', '006 Tonal Adjustment', '007 Sharpen Eyes', '008 Soften Skin', and '009 High Contrast'. With each audible mouse click, a preset is selected, triggering the warning dialog box, which the user consistently dismisses by clicking 'Yes'. This action causes the image to visibly transform with noticeable changes in color temperature, contrast, and brightness. The speaker explains he's demonstrating what the presets do and that he uses them as "starting points." He then clicks on the 'High Contrast' preset, observing that while it might be "a little bit more contrast" than desired, it provides a good "idea" and "points me in the right direction." The right panel of the interface remains static, displaying adjustment options like Loupe, Histogram, and White Balance, while the bottom status bar shows file details '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, along with camera specifics (24.0 MP, ISO 800, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON Z 7). 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons are also visible.

The video segment opens with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker begins by explaining that if a user likes what a preset, such as the currently selected '009 High Contrast', is doing, they can simply click the 'save' or 'apply' button in the lower right corner. He then clarifies that often, after selecting a preset, users will want to access their 'Global Adjustments', which he notes he had previously closed. As he speaks, the user clicks on the 'Global Adjustments' section in the right panel, revealing sliders for Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, Structure, Warmth, and color channels. The speaker demonstrates how to make changes, specifically mentioning reducing contrast if it's too much. Visually, the user adjusts the Contrast slider, increasing it from 37% to 39% and then decreasing it back to 37%. The speaker then announces his intention to move back to the left-hand side of the interface, explaining he wants to cover those tools first before discussing the right-hand side. Following this, the user clicks on the 'Custom' section in the left panel, which expands to show two custom presets: 'Color IR' and 'High Key'. The speaker states he has just created these two custom presets, and the user hovers over 'Color IR', displaying a thumbnail of a purple-toned image, though it is not selected.

The video opens with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a close-up of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker begins by explaining he has created his own presets, specifically mentioning "color infrared" and "high key" as examples. He demonstrates by interacting with the 'Presets' panel on the left, selecting 'Color IR'. A dark-themed warning dialog box appears, stating that applying the preset will replace all current parameters, which the speaker confirms by clicking 'Yes'. The image instantly transforms into a surreal infrared effect, with the foliage turning deep red and the flowers appearing vibrant purple, which the speaker describes as a "false color infrared representation" and an "interesting aesthetic" from "playing with the software." He then selects the 'High Key' preset, again triggering the same warning dialog, which he confirms by clicking 'Yes'. The image reverts to a bright, high-key version with enhanced whites and lighter greens, which the speaker notes might be "too high key." To refine it, he manually adjusts the 'Brightness' slider in the 'Global Adjustments' panel, reducing it from 11% to -6%, and subsequently lowers the 'Highlights' slider in the 'Selective Tones' section from 35% to 15%, explaining his intent to "take my highlights back down" and "dial it in where I want it to be" using the right side of the interface. Throughout these manual adjustments and preset applications, the interface consistently displays the bottom status bar indicating the file '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, along with camera details (NIKON Z 7, ISO 800), and the 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons. Towards the end of the segment, the speaker further elaborates on the functionality of the presets, discussing the ability to export them from the left-hand side of the interface for use on other computers or for sharing.

The segment opens within the dark-themed Viveza 3 photo editing interface, displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker provides a running commentary, initially explaining how to export and save custom presets, then demonstrating how to trash or delete them using a small trash can icon on the left panel. He transitions to discussing how to 're-up or update' an existing preset. Visually, the user's cursor interacts with the 'High Key' preset thumbnail, first showing a tooltip to 'export this preset', then changing to 'update this preset with the current settings' upon hover. The speaker elaborates, stating, "let's say I prefer the settings... that we've just made to the original settings." As the user clicks the 'High Key' preset, a 'Warning' dialog box appears centrally, asking, "Are you sure you want to replace this preset?" The speaker anticipates this, saying, "it's going to say, are you sure? And um, I'm just going to say yes and voila, now I've updated my high key preset. Good to go." The user visually confirms by clicking 'Yes', making the dialog box disappear. The interface returns to its normal state, with the right panel displaying global and selective tone adjustments, including Brightness at -6%, Saturation at -10%, Highlights at 15%, Midtones at 31%, and Shadows at 8%. The bottom status bar confirms the file '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with NIKON Z 7 camera details. The speaker continues to discuss importing and exporting presets for use across different devices.

event_blocks.summary: The Viveza 3 photo editing interface displays a close-up of green foliage with white flowers, with the DxO PhotoLab status bar showing '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from a NIKON Z 7 at ISO 800. Initially, the speaker navigates the 'Presets' section, explaining preset import and selecting '000 Neutral' via a warning dialog box, resetting the image. He then critiques the image as flat with distracting dead sticks and lacking depth, describing it as a two-dimensional plane. Transitioning to editing, the speaker discusses creating depth through color contrast, intending to darken greens and warm flowers. For 35 seconds, global and selective tone sliders remain at 0%. At 0:36, the user clicks 'Histogram', expanding it to reveal RGB and L channels, and a 'Loupe' preview appears, dynamically updating with cursor movement. The speaker explains the 'live histogram' and demonstrates cycling through RGB and Luminance channels, highlighting clipped areas with 'Show clipped shadows' and 'Show clipped highlights' overlays, identified as magenta or blue. After collapsing the histogram, the speaker introduces 'Global Adjustments' (Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, Structure, Shadow, Warmth, Red, Green, Blue, Hue) and 'Selective Tones' (Highlights, Midtones, Shadows, Blacks), noting the latter are new. The user then adjusts the 'Highlights' slider to -45%, visibly darkening the brightest areas.

Viveza 3 Interface.description: A photo editing environment with a full-screen view of a foliage image, featuring a left panel with presets and a right panel with adjustment options. The user interacts with the 'Histogram' section, cycling through RGB, Red, Green, Blue, and Luminance channels. The 'Loupe' preview is active, and the user toggles 'Show clipped shadows' and 'Show clipped highlights', which overlay purple and blue indicators on the image. All adjustment sliders remain at 0% throughout, except for the 'Highlights' slider, which is adjusted to -45%. The bottom status bar indicates the file being modified is '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (24.0 MP, ISO 800, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON Z 7). The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons are visible at the bottom right, and the Nik Collection logo is consistently displayed in the bottom right corner. Its features and functionality are being explained by a clear male voice throughout the segment.

Warning Dialog Box.description: A dark-themed pop-up window appearing in the center of the screen with the message 'Are you sure you want to replace this preset?' and two buttons: 'No' (gray) and 'Yes' (orange). The speaker explicitly says "say yes" as he visually confirms the action by clicking the orange 'Yes' button, which occurs once during the segment for the 'High Key' preset.

DxO PhotoLab 4 Interface.description: A dark-themed photo editing software interface displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers, featuring panels for histogram, metadata, and basic adjustment tools on the left and right sides, with the image centered in the workspace. The Metadata panel is expanded and collapsed during the segment, revealing detailed EXIF information including camera model (NIKON Z 7), ISO 800, shutter speed 1/200 s, focal length 70 mm, and file path. The Basic Tools panel remains unchanged with all sliders at default values. The History panel at the bottom shows thumbnails of the current image and two other files. The Nik Collection logo is consistently visible in the bottom right corner, and the 'Export to Disk' button is present at the bottom right of the interface.

raw_segments: The video opens on the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, displaying a close-up of green foliage with white flowers. The left panel shows the 'Presets' section, including 'Custom' presets like 'Color IR' and 'High Key'. A male speaker explains how to import presets using an export button, moving them between computers. He then states his intention to "hide the presets over here on the left side" but first wants to "go back to neutral." He scrolls to the top of the 'Imported' section, revealing a list of presets such as '007 Sharpen Eyes', '006 Soften Skin', and '000 Neutral'. As he selects the '000 Neutral' preset, a dark-themed warning dialog box appears in the center of the screen, asking "Are you sure you want to replace this preset?". The speaker says "hit neutral, say yes," and visually clicks the orange 'Yes' button, causing the image to reset to its original state with all adjustment sliders in the right panel returning to 0%. The bottom status bar consistently displays the file information: '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (24.0 MP, ISO 800, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON Z 7). The speaker then describes the image as "a little bit flat" and notes that "photographically, there isn't any direct area of interest," with the subject being "kind of the entire image." He points out "distracting elements with the kind of dead sticks" that he "probably should clean those up," expressing a desire to "create some more depth in the photograph." He concludes by stating the image "has no foreground or middle ground background. It's just two-dimensional plane." The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons remain visible at the bottom right.

The segment opens with a continuous view of the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker explains the goal of creating depth in the image using color contrast, detailing how 'control points' will be used to enhance green areas, making them darker and more contrasted, and how warmer-toned flowers will be warmed up further to create separation from the cooler greens. For the first 35 seconds, the interface remains static, with all 'Global Adjustments' and 'Selective Tones' sliders set to 0%, indicating no edits have been applied. The status bar at the bottom confirms the file being edited is '_DSC0088_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, captured with a NIKON Z 7 at ISO 800. As the speaker transitions to discussing the right-hand side of the interface, at 0:36, the user interacts by clicking the 'Histogram' option, which expands to reveal a color histogram with RGB and L (luminance) channels. Simultaneously, the 'Loupe' preview appears above the histogram, displaying a magnified section of the image. The speaker describes how this 'Loupe feature' will be open and follow the cursor, which is then visually demonstrated as the user moves the cursor across the main image, causing the Loupe to dynamically update and the histogram to reflect the tonal values of the selected area. The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons remain visible at the bottom right, and the interface maintains its dark theme with orange accents, with the speaker concluding by mentioning closing something down to avoid distraction.

The video opens on the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers. A male speaker immediately begins explaining the "live histogram" feature, noting it changes with adjustments, though no global adjustments are made visually. He then guides the viewer through the histogram panel on the right, demonstrating how to view different channels. event_blocks.summary: The DxO PhotoLab 4 Interface initially displays a close-up of green foliage, with a speaker explaining image organization in PhotoLab or Lightroom. A landscape image of a lake and farm is then selected, noted as lacking a strong foreground and having flat contrast, particularly in the clouds. A Plugin Selector Dialog prompts the user to choose 'Viveza 3', transitioning the interface. A Warning Dialog Box asking 'Are you sure you want to replace this preset?' is confirmed with 'Yes'. The speaker then guides the user to add control points, starting with one on the sky, increasing 'Brightness' to 100% to brighten it. The Nik Collection logo is consistently visible. The user then places a control point on the sky of the landscape image and increases 'Structure' to 92% to enhance cloud texture, followed by increasing 'Saturation' to 64% for vividness, and 'Red' to 25% for warmth, which the speaker explains neutralizes cyan in the clouds. The status bar shows 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, captured with a Nikon D850. The speaker explains grouping control points using a folder icon or 'Command G' shortcut, allowing simultaneous adjustments; the user groups three points and adjusts 'Structure' to 92% and 'Red' to 25%, with 'Saturation' at 64%. The speaker then focuses on creating layering and cooler tones in the trees, adding 'Control Point 4' and adjusting 'Brightness' to -16%, 'Structure' to 30%, and 'Warmth' to -31%. 'Control Point 5' is placed on the farm, increasing 'Contrast' to 17% and 'Saturation' to 66%. The speaker demonstrates duplicating control points using 'Option click and drag' to apply adjustments to other areas, like the farm and distant mountains, accentuating photographic layering for depth. The user refines adjustments, for example, increasing 'Contrast' to 17% and 'Structure' to 16% for one point, and reducing 'Brightness' to -16% while increasing 'Saturation' to 30% for another. The speaker highlights Viveza 3's ability to change colors, as the user collapses the 'Control Points' panel. The speaker explains control points make clean selections, and the user refines 'Control Point 10' strength from 20% to 14% using 'Color Selectivity' sliders, including 'Luminance' and 'Chrominance', to achieve a more precise selection, previewing in monochrome. The user adjusts 'Luminance' for the farm area from 50% to 100% and then back down to 65%, with slight 'Chrominance' adjustments. The speaker then demonstrates using the 'Color Picker' to change the barn's color, selecting a blue hue and applying it, then adjusting 'Brightness' to -46%, 'Luminance' to 38%, and 'Chrominance' to 65%. The user duplicates control points, setting 'Brightness' to -65% for 'Control Point 11' and 'Control Point 12' near the white house, and refines 'Control Point 10' with 'Luminance' at 65% and 'Chrominance' at 45%, adding further points to the house and roof. The Nik Collection logo is consistently visible in the bottom right corner throughout these detailed editing processes.

A male voice, likely an instructor, guides the viewer through the process, explaining the purpose of duplicating and moving control points, the importance of honing luminance and chrominance values, and the distinction between selection-based and object-based adjustments, specifically mentioning the 'barn itself' as a focus for precise adjustments.

raw_segments: The video opens with the DxO PhotoLab 4 Interface displaying a close-up image of green foliage with white flowers, while a male speaker explains how original images are saved and organized within PhotoLab or Adobe Lightroom, appearing next to each other by default. The Metadata panel is expanded, showing detailed EXIF information including NIKON Z 7, ISO 800, 1/200 s shutter speed, and 70 mm focal length. The user then selects a different image from the History panel, a landscape photo of a lake with a farm. The speaker introduces this as "our next image," noting it "could use some layering" and, despite having a foreground, middle ground, and background, lacks a "real strong foreground element" and has "overall contrast that is a little bit flat," particularly in the clouds, due to atmospheric conditions and a lack of "tonal contrast." A Plugin Selector Dialog appears, from which the user selects 'Viveza 3'. The interface transitions to the Viveza 3 Interface, displaying the landscape image in full screen. A Warning Dialog Box then appears, asking 'Are you sure you want to replace this preset?', and the user clicks 'Yes'. The speaker then states, "Let's add some control points," as the Viveza 3 interface activates the 'Control Points' panel. The user places a control point on the sky area and visibly brightens the sky by increasing the Brightness slider to 100%. The Nik Collection logo remains consistently visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

The video segment features a user editing a landscape photograph within the Viveza 3 interface, part of the Nik Collection. The image displays a serene scene with a lake, a green hillside, trees, and a farm with a red barn and white house under a cloudy sky. A male voice is heard stating, "control point into the sky, and let's just let's just boom out the structure." The user places a control point on the sky area and adjusts the 'Structure' slider in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel, increasing it from 0% to 92%. The speaker explains, "the structure tool here is going to bring that texture... bring out the contrast in the texture that is in the cloud, we're just accentuating it." He acknowledges that "rarely are you going to add control points and add 90% structure... for the sake of our demonstration, it does add quite a lot of impact." Following this, the speaker announces, "we're going to add a little bit of saturation," and the user increases the 'Saturation' slider to 64% to make the colors more vivid. The speaker then states, "I think I'm going to actually add a little bit of red," as the user adjusts the 'Red' slider from 0% to 25% to add warmth and richness, likely affecting the barn. The speaker clarifies that "what adding red is going to do is it's going to remove cyan, and it'll actually make those clouds look a little bit more... kind of neutral." Throughout the segment, the Nik Collection logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and the status bar indicates the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, captured with a Nikon D850 at ISO 100. The 'Apply' and 'Cancel' buttons remain visible, but the user does not apply the changes.

The video segment showcases a continuous workflow within the Viveza 3 photo editing software, where an expert voice guides the viewer through selective adjustments on a landscape image featuring a lake, green hills, and a farm under a cloudy sky. The speaker explains that having previously bumped up saturation (mentioning 54%, though the visual currently displays 64%) accentuates the clouds. Visually, the user places a control point on the sky and adjusts the 'Structure' slider to 92%, 'Saturation' to 64%, and 'Red' to 25% in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel, enhancing the texture and vibrancy of the clouds and adding warmth. The speaker describes this as a nice technique to bring out those tones. Subsequently, the user adds two more control points, expanding the localized adjustment area across the sky with curved lines connecting the points. The speaker then details how to duplicate control points on a Mac by holding the Option key (or Alt on a PC) while clicking and dragging, explaining that this allows for placing points in other cloud areas to achieve more impact. The Viveza 3 interface remains consistent, with the 'Control Points' panel active, and the bottom status bar confirms the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with NIKON D850 camera details (ISO 100). The Nik Collection logo is visible in the bottom right corner throughout, while the 'Apply' button remains unclicked, indicating ongoing adjustments.

The video opens on the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, showcasing a landscape image of a lake, a farm, and distant mountains under a cloudy sky. A male speaker immediately begins explaining the process of controlling multiple adjustment points simultaneously, stating, "We can actually group these together and then make adjustments to the group of control points at once." The user visually places three control points in the sky area, which are then highlighted in gold as the speaker instructs to "hold the shift key down" to select multiple points, confirming their active status. The speaker then guides the viewer to the right-hand side of the interface, identifying a "little folder and then a little crosshair" as "our group button," also mentioning the keyboard shortcut "command G." The user clicks this group icon, effectively grouping the three control points into 'Group 1'. The speaker confirms, "that groups them," and explains that now, adjusting one control point will "adjust all of them." Subsequently, the user adjusts the 'Structure' slider to 92% and the 'Red' slider to 25% within the 'Basic Adjustments' panel, while 'Saturation' remains at 64%. The bottom status bar consistently displays the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, along with camera details (24.0 MP, ISO 100, NIKON CORPORATION, NIKON D850), and the Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner.

The segment opens with a user actively editing a landscape photograph within the Viveza 3 interface, part of the Nik Collection. A male voice narrates, highlighting the utility of control points as a "really nice feature" built into Viveza and other Nik plugins for grouping adjustments. The image, featuring a lake, green hillside, trees, and a farm with a red barn and white house under a cloudy sky, is being refined. The user first adjusts the 'Structure' slider in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel for 'Group 1' to 79%. Following this, the narrator expresses a desire to create "a little bit of layering" in the trees, aiming to "darken down some of the shadow areas just a touch" and "make them a little bit cooler." The user then adds 'Control Point 4' over the trees on the left, adjusting its 'Brightness' to -16%, 'Structure' to 30%, 'Warmth' to -31%, and 'Blue' to 0%. The narrator explains that instead of using red, green, and blue sliders, he'll "just move to the warmth slider and I'll remove some warmth," noting it "does something similar" and is "easy and fast" though offering "a little bit less control" than RGB. He then points out "deciduous trees here that are behind this line here right on the kind of coastline or the beach." Subsequently, 'Control Point 5' is placed over the farm area, with its 'Contrast' increased to 17% and 'Saturation' to 66%. As the user adjusts sliders for Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, Structure, Shadow, and Warmth in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel—for instance, increasing Contrast to 17% and Structure to 16% for one point, and reducing Brightness to -16% while increasing Saturation to 30% for another—the speaker elaborates that these actions are "accentuating the photographic layering" to achieve "more depth within the photograph." The segment opens with a male voice explaining how control points in Viveza 3 are designed to make "really clean selections" on specific "object or an area." Visually, the user is actively working within the Viveza 3 photo editing software, displaying a landscape image of a lake, green hills, and a farm. Multiple control points are being placed across the sky and landscape. The speaker references a "red barn" as an example where a control point can achieve a "really nice clean selection," describing the process of placing a control point and potentially needing to "size the area of influence." He then evaluates the current selection, noting it's "pretty good" but might be "selecting some of the green in the foreground" which he might not want. This aligns with the visual action where the user selects 'Control Point 10' and precisely adjusts its strength from 20% to 14%, aiming to refine its placement and influence. To achieve "a more precise selection," the speaker introduces the "color selectivity sliders." Concurrently, the user toggles the 'Color Picker' tool, immediately switching the image to a black-and-white luminance preview, and begins adjusting the 'Luminance' and 'Chrominance' sliders under 'Color Selectivity' to refine the selection. The speaker emphasizes that these "brand new" sliders in the "newest version of Viveza" allow users to "hone in on the selection." The 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons remain visible as the segment concludes with the speaker beginning to explain how to use these new features, stating, "to use them, you can either tell the control point..."

The segment opens within the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, displaying a dark, low-light image of a farm with a barn and silo. A male speaker immediately begins explaining the tools, clarifying that "luminance would be brightness" and "chrominance would be the color values." As the speaker explains that sliding the 'Luminance' slider to the right "hones in more," making the selection smaller, the user first increases the 'Luminance' slider under 'Color Selectivity' from 50% to 100%, visibly brightening the selected farm area. The speaker then instructs, "Let me actually double click here. I'm going to zoom in, um, one to two," and the user follows by adjusting the zoom level from 1:1 to 1:2, revealing more detail. The speaker continues, explaining that sliding the 'Luminance' slider to the left "broadens out the selection," making the tolerance wider. The user then reduces the 'Luminance' slider back down to 0%, causing the image to darken again, before increasing it to 65% to achieve a balanced exposure for the farm. A slight adjustment is also made to the 'Chrominance' slider. Throughout these precise and deliberate actions, the bottom status bar confirms the file being edited is 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (NIKON D850, ISO 100), and the Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner. The speaker concludes by emphasizing the use of a combination of these sliders depending on the selection.

The video opens with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a dark, almost black farm scene, with the 'Control Points' panel active on the right. A male voice states he's "actually pretty happy with what's happening," explaining, "I'm using the luminance slider, and then I'm also using the chrominance slider to really hone in the selection." As he speaks, the user adjusts the 'Luminance' slider under 'Color Selectivity' for 'Control Point 10' from 38% to 65%, while 'Chrominance' remains at 65%. The video opens with a user interacting with the Viveza 3 photo editing software, displaying an outdoor landscape image featuring water, green trees, and a farm with a silo. The segment opens with a male voice explaining the strategic placement of control points within the Viveza 3 interface, suggesting duplicating a control point to apply adjustments to "other things that we want blue." event_blocks.summary: The video begins with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a farm landscape, where a male speaker comments on editing, adjusting the view to fit the screen and noting a "blue barn now." He suggests moving to "Color Effects Pro" for a gradient and flourishes. After adjusting zoom and observing the DxO PhotoLab 4 status bar with file details, the speaker initiates the 'Apply' process, leading to a 'Processing image' progress bar. The scene shifts to DxO PhotoLab 4, showing the same image with a histogram and metadata panel, where the speaker discusses previous "dramatic adjustments." The user then accesses EXIF data in Photoshop 2021, showing a twilight image of a woman by water, illuminated by a "large flash" strobe. A black studio lamp is visible as the light source. The speaker explains David Turner's technique of gelling a flash with CTO plastic for separation. The session transitions to Photoshop 2021, where the speaker discusses using a 'clone stamp' to remove a 'light source' and prepares to enter Viveza via the 'Nik selective tool' or the 'Filter' dropdown. After flattening the image, the Viveza 3 interface appears with presets and adjustment panels. The speaker aims to "massage the color a little bit," adding a control point on the woman's face and adjusting 'Warmth' to -100%, then fine-tuning 'Red' to -21% and 'Warmth' to -34% to avoid a gray skin tone. Additional control points are added to her torso and lower body, with a mistake noted in adding an extra point, which is then deleted via a "Warning Dialog Box." The 'Structure' slider is adjusted to 51% for texture and contrast, and 'Brightness' is increased to 8% on her face. A control point is added to the sky, adjusting 'Brightness' to -24%, 'Saturation' to 38%, and 'Structure' to 35%. Another control point is deleted after brief adjustment. The speaker explains an "in-camera" trick using white balance and an "orange gel light" for color separation, relating it to Viveza's function and advising to "get the best that you can out of the camera, you shoot in raw." The 'Compare' mode is activated, showing before-and-after views with a split preview. Zooming in, the speaker notes neutralizing some of the original skin tone for separation. A new control point on the woman's face has 'Structure' increased to 73% and 'Red' decreased to -28% to separate her hair and reduce redness. The changes are applied, with the speaker expressing satisfaction with the color. The user duplicates the 'Viveza 3' layer, and Lori asks Dan about using Viveza versus Color Effects, to which Dan replies it's a matter of preference.

Adobe Photoshop 2021.description: A photo editing software interface displaying the image of the woman standing on rocks by the water during twilight. The status bar at the bottom indicates the file being modified is 'DT_MG_0014-4.psd' from Adobe Photoshop 2021. The interface is embedded within the Viveza 3 plugin, with no visible Photoshop panels or tools outside the Viveza workspace.

Viveza 3 Interface.description: A photo editing environment with a full-screen view of the image of the woman by the water. The interface displays a panel on the right with 'Selective Adjustments' expanded, showing 'Control Points' with multiple control points being added and adjusted. The 'Basic Adjustments' sliders for Brightness, Contrast, Saturation, Structure, Shadow, Warmth, Red, Green, Blue, and Hue are visible and interacted with. The Nik Collection logo is consistently displayed in the bottom right corner, and the 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons are visible at the bottom right. A male speaker (Dan) refers to it as the 'Viveza application' and discusses its 'technique' for controlling 'color and light' and its historical use for 'CEO portraits.' raw_segments: The video begins with the Viveza 3 interface displaying a landscape image of a farm by a lake, featuring multiple Control Points. A male speaker comments on editing techniques, stating "it works pretty well" and noting a "blue barn now" after adjusting the view to "fit to screen." He suggests finishing the image by bringing it into "Color Effects Pro" to add a "gradient" and "flourishes" using filters. The user adjusts the zoom from 1:1 to 1:2, revealing finer details of the farm buildings, while the right panel shows the 'Control Points' list and the bottom status bar indicates the file 'RIT_7357_Nik.tiff' from DxO PhotoLab, with camera details (NIKON D850). The speaker announces, "For the sake of time, we're going to click the apply button," and the user clicks 'Apply', triggering a 'Processing image' progress bar. The scene transitions to DxO PhotoLab 4, displaying the same landscape image with a histogram and metadata panel. The speaker explains that previous images, processed within Viveza, involved "dramatic adjustments" and "a ton of different things." The user clicks on the metadata panel, revealing detailed EXIF information (NIKON Z 7, ISO 800, 1/200 s, 70 mm). The video then switches to Adobe Photoshop 2021, showing a different image of a woman on rocks by the water during twilight. The speaker announces, "I'm going to jump over into Photoshop," and takes a sip of water, remarking "my throat's getting dry." He then describes this new image as an "outdoor photograph" that is "illuminated using a strobe flash," specifically "a large flash." The Photoshop interface remains static at 50% zoom, showing the Layers, Navigator, and History panels, with no edits made.

The segment opens with a static view of Adobe Photoshop 2021, displaying an image of a woman on rocky terrain by the water at twilight, zoomed to 50%. The Layers panel shows an empty 'Layer 1' and the 'Background' image, while the Navigator and History panels are also visible. A male speaker confirms they are "over here in Photoshop" after switching host software. At 0:08, a black studio-style lamp with a glowing bulb appears in the top-right of the image canvas, which the speaker identifies as "the light source that's actually illuminating the model here." He explains that this photograph, featuring a serene coastal scene with distant boats under a dusky blue sky, was shot by his friend and colleague David Turner for a Maine media workshop. The speaker details the portrait lighting technique David used, involving setting the camera's white balance to an incandescent light source and gelling a flash "like this" with a CTO (color temperature orange) piece of plastic to create separation between the subject and background. The floating lamp, casting no visible light or shadow, serves as a visual representation of this technique rather than an active edit. The Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right, and the speaker concludes by stating their next step will be to use Viveza, a Nik Collection plugin, to refine the colors.

The segment opens with Adobe Photoshop 2021 displaying an image of a woman on rocks by the water at twilight, with the Layers panel showing an empty 'Layer 1' and the 'Background' image. A male speaker explains a past edit, mentioning using a 'clone stamp' to remove a 'light source' from 'Layer 1' as a "behind the scenes" detail, while visually, a black, dome-shaped studio lamp with a glowing bulb appears as a stationary overlay in the top-right of the canvas, not affecting the image. The speaker then announces the intention to "jump into Viveza," first demonstrating access via the "Nik selective tool," which brings up a dark-themed Plugin Selector Dialog listing various Nik Collection plugins, including 'Viveza 3'. He then shows an alternative method, navigating to the 'Filter' dropdown menu in the top portion of the interface, selecting 'Nik Collection', and choosing 'Viveza 3'. Before launching, the speaker states his intention to "flatten my image" to avoid problems. Upon selection, the Viveza 3 interface launches, presenting the same image with a left panel displaying presets like '000 Neutral', '001 Golden Hour', and '002 Blue Hour', and a right panel for 'Global Adjustments', 'Selective Adjustments', and 'Control Points'. As the user hovers over the presets, the image updates in real-time, demonstrating the 'Golden Hour' and 'Blue Hour' effects, confirming the speaker's earlier statement that the software would look "exactly the same." The Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout.

The video opens on the Viveza 3 photo editing interface, integrated with Adobe Photoshop 2021, displaying an image of a woman on rocky terrain by the water at twilight, with sailboats under a deep blue sky. A male speaker explains his goal to "massage the color a little bit." He selects the 'Control Points' tool under 'Selective Adjustments' and places a circular control point on the woman’s face, noting she appears "a little bit warmer than intended." He then adjusts the 'Warmth' slider in the 'Basic Adjustments' panel, reducing it significantly to -100%, which visibly cools her skin tone, while cautioning that removing "too much" warmth would make her skin "go gray," which was "not what David was after" for "nice color separation." He fine-tunes the adjustment by modifying the 'Red' slider to -21% and then the 'Warmth' slider again to -34%, emphasizing the need to "be careful" not to shift colors too far. Subsequently, he adds a second control point on the woman’s torso and then a third on her lower body, explaining he'll "drop a couple points," before realizing he made a "mistake" by adding an unwanted "third or fourth control point." Throughout, the interface remains focused on the 'Control Points' panel, with the Nik Collection logo consistently visible in the bottom right corner.

The video segment opens within the Viveza 3 plugin, embedded in Adobe Photoshop 2021, displaying an image of a woman on rocky terrain by the water at twilight. A male speaker immediately states his intention to delete the fourth control point, explaining, "I don't want the fourth control point there. So I need to delete it," and clarifies that an active control point is "highlighted in gold or yellow." Visually, Control Point 4 is selected, and the speaker instructs the user to "move over to the right-hand side of the tools palette, click the delete button," which is then performed. This action triggers a "Warning Dialog Box" asking to confirm deletion, which the user accepts. The speaker also mentions that the delete key on the keyboard is a valid shortcut. Subsequently, the speaker announces, "So, let's add a little structure," as Control Point 3 is selected and the 'Structure' slider is adjusted to 51%. The speaker elaborates that 'Structure' is "basically a texture adjustment" and a "contrast adjustment" that "aims to separate the darker tones from the lighter tones" to "increase that separation and sort of texture." Finally, Control Point 1, positioned over the woman’s face, is selected. The speaker notes he's "still removing too much warmth on her skin" as the 'Warmth' slider is adjusted to -21%, then adds, "Let's even brighten her up just a little bit," as the 'Brightness' slider is increased to 8%. Throughout this selective editing process, the Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, along with the 'Apply', 'Cancel', and 'Save preset' buttons, with no changes to the image’s background or overall lighting.

The video segment captures a continuous photo editing session within the Viveza 3 plugin, embedded in Adobe Photoshop 2021, displaying an image of a woman on rocky terrain by a body of water during twilight. A male voice narrates the process, initially mentioning "Just 10%" in relation to an adjustment. The editor actively uses the 'Selective Adjustments' panel, focusing on 'Control Points'. The narrator suggests, "And then drop a control point maybe in the blue sky," and later, "Let's see what happens if we add some uh structure to the blue sky, just a little," and "Maybe darken down the background just a touch." Visually, Control Point 5 is selected, and its 'Brightness' is adjusted to -24%, 'Saturation' to 38%, and 'Structure' to 35%. Subsequently, a new Control Point (Control Point 7) is added near the woman’s feet but then deleted after a brief adjustment. The editor then selects Control Point 6, adjusting its 'Structure' to 35% and 'Contrast' to 5%. Throughout these visual manipulations, the narrator elaborates on a "dramatic color difference" technique using white balance, explaining how setting camera white balance to incandescent and using an "orange gel light" can result in a "near correct" skin tone while the background goes "blue" – an "in-camera" trick. He then connects this to the current activity, stating, "what we're doing here with Viveza is just kind of honing in the tool a bit," before concluding with general advice to "get the best that you can out of the camera, you shoot in raw." The 'Apply' and 'Cancel' buttons remain visible at the bottom, and the Nik Collection logo is consistently displayed in the bottom right corner.

The segment opens with the Viveza 3 interface displaying an image of a woman on rocky terrain by the water at twilight, with sailboats in the background. The 'Selective Adjustments' panel is visible on the right, listing several existing Control Points. A male speaker explains the process of bringing images into post-processing to massage tones. The user then activates the 'Compare' mode, which the speaker introduces as the "third and final compare button," splitting the screen vertically to show a before-and-after view, then switching to a horizontal split. The speaker elaborates on this, stating they've looked at the before and after using side-by-side, and that the split preview allows for a complete split that can be slid back and forth. The user zooms in to 1:3, focusing on the woman, as the speaker comments on her "really nice skin tone originally" and how they "neutralized a little bit of it to create a little more separation." A new Control Point (Control Point 8) is added on her face, with the speaker suggesting adding "one more control point" and zooming in. The settings for this new Control Point are adjusted, with the 'Structure' slider increased to 73% and the 'Red' slider decreased to -28%, which the speaker explains will help her hair separate and remove a "little bit of red" if it's too warm. The user then clicks the 'Apply' button to confirm the changes, while the speaker expresses, "I like the color personally." Throughout the segment, the status bar at the bottom indicates the file is being modified from Adobe Photoshop 2021, and the Nik Collection logo remains visible in the bottom right corner.

The video opens with the Viveza 3 interface embedded within Adobe Photoshop 2021, displaying an image of a woman standing on rocks by the water during twilight. A male speaker, later identified as Dan, describes the 'technique' as 'very specific' and an 'interesting tool' for controlling 'color and light' both 'in camera' and 'afterwards' using the 'Viveza application.' As Dan speaks, the user adds a new Control Point (Control Point 9) on the woman’s lower leg and adjusts its settings, increasing Brightness to 28% and decreasing Red to -26% to warm up the skin tone. Dan then states he's 'going to click apply in the lower right corner,' and the user applies the changes, returning to the main Photoshop workspace where the Viveza 3 adjustment is now visible as a new layer. The user proceeds to duplicate this layer, creating a copy named 'Viveza 3 copy', likely to refine or experiment with different edits. A female speaker, Lori, then addresses Dan, asking about a question from 'Alan' regarding when to use 'Viveza instead of Color Effects,' to which Dan responds that it 'comes down to what you prefer.'

event_blocks.summary: The Adobe Photoshop 2021 interface initially displayed a twilight image of a woman on rocks, featuring the Nik Collection plugin panel with Viveza 3 highlighted and an active 'Viveza 3' layer. A male speaker expressed a preference for Viveza 3's control points and its color adjustments, contrasting it with Color Efex for broader aesthetic changes. Following a female speaker's interjection about negative control points, the visual abruptly shifted to a sunlit photograph of a historic stone bridge. The editing session then focused on the Viveza 3 plugin, where the speaker elaborated on control points, demonstrating their use by adding multiple points to a yellow building facade. The 'Saturation' slider was increased to +100% for these points, intensely coloring the targeted areas. The speaker explained how these 'constricting control points' hone selections and can be adjusted to create effects like 'checkerboarding' with varying saturation levels, emphasizing their interaction and the ability to 'shrink' their influence. Throughout this process, the Nik Collection logo remained visible, and adjustments were previewed without final application.

The video opens with the Adobe Photoshop 2021 interface displaying a sunlit photograph of a historic stone bridge, while a male speaker immediately begins explaining the concept of control points, specifically 'negative control points' in Viveza. As the speaker elaborates on adding 'another control point,' the view transitions to the full-screen Viveza 3 interface, still showing the bridge scene, which the speaker refers to as 'John's photograph.' The user then activates the 'Control Points' tool under 'Selective Adjustments' and places the first control point on a yellow building facade. Concurrently, the speaker states, 'I'm going to add a ton of saturation,' and the 'Saturation' slider is visually adjusted to +100%, intensely coloring the building. The speaker continues to explain how 'this control point is going in and affecting this area a little bit,' as the user adds a second and then a third control point to different sections of the building, leaving their basic adjustments at default. The speaker further illustrates how adding another control point can 'neutralize' an area, and later introduces the term 'constrictive control points,' before briefly mentioning the potential for a 'rabbit hole' when discussing 'color effects pro.' The video opens on an Adobe Photoshop 2021 editing session within the Viveza 3 plugin, displaying a sunlit photograph of a historic stone bridge with buildings and a small boat. A male voice explains the functionality of control points, noting how they allow for applying individual filters, acting as both "plus" and "minus" points for "color effects," either "putting the filter in or taking it out." He emphasizes that in Viveza, these "constricting control points" are used to "hone in the selections" and are not just negative, but "able to make other adjustments." As he speaks about removing and adding saturation to create "interesting layering," the user methodically adds control points to the yellow building facade. Initially, three are visible, and the user adds a fourth, then a fifth, and finally a sixth, each targeting different sections. The speaker continues, describing how one might "shrink that control point" and "place one over here," leading to a "checkerboarding effect" where some areas are "highly saturated" and others are not, demonstrating how "they are working with each other." Visually, the user adjusts the 'Saturation' slider for Control Points 4, 5, and 6 to 100%, visibly intensifying the yellow tones and enhancing the vibrancy of the targeted building sections. The 'Selective Adjustments' panel with 'Control Points' expanded is visible, and the Nik Collection logo remains in the bottom right corner throughout this precise, localized color enhancement process.
